name: The Dual Reader Standard — Complete Guide
headline: One-Pass Guide to the Entire Dual Reader Standard: Architecture, Protocols, Kernel, Layers, Compatibility, Lingua Franca, and Adoption
description: Comprehensive guide to the Dual Reader Standard (DRS), a verification architecture requiring every claim to be readable by both human and machine. Covers both protocols (DRP for text, GVP for software), the Falsification Kernel (Layer 0), the AI layer artifact, three claim types, six verification tiers, eight inference rules, the six-condition phase gate, three-axis compatibility, the machine lingua franca property (embedded binary logic), and self-spreading adoption dynamics.

about.name: Dual Reader Standard
about.description: A verification architecture for knowledge systems requiring every claim to be readable by two independent reader classes — human and machine — each operating through a defined protocol.

isPartOf.name: Fracttalix — Dual Reader Standard Implementation Guides

section_01_what_is_the_drs.title: What the Dual Reader Standard Is
section_01_what_is_the_drs.summary: The DRS is a verification architecture for knowledge systems. It requires every claim — whether written in prose or implemented in code — to be readable by two independent reader classes: human and machine. It is not a paper or a tool. It is the standard that contains both of its protocols.

DRP.name: Dual Reader Protocol
DRP.function: Makes prose claims machine-evaluable via 5-part falsification predicates

GVP.name: Grounded Verification Protocol
GVP.function: Makes machine-evaluable claims machine-verified via test bindings and commit-pinned evidence

section_01_what_is_the_drs.relationship: The DRP makes claims machine-evaluable. The GVP makes them machine-verified. Neither is complete without the other. Together they are the DRS.

DRP.name: Dual Reader Protocol (Text)
DRP.description: Governs how prose claims become machine-evaluable.

readers.reader_1_human: Reads the paper in natural language. Understands context, motivation, and narrative. Cannot systematically audit every claim.
readers.reader_2_ai: Reads the AI layer — a structured JSON document that accompanies every paper. Contains the full claim registry. Can audit every claim without reading prose.

requirements: Claim classification: every claim typed as A (axiom), D (definition), or F (falsifiable)
Falsification predicates: every Type F claim carries a 5-part deterministic predicate
Phase gates: six conditions (c1–c6) before PHASE-READY status
Placeholder tracking: claims depending on unresolved results are registered as placeholders
DRP.guarantee: Any AI system with access to the AI layer can evaluate any falsifiable claim without reading the prose. Self-sufficiency enforced at phase gate condition c5.

GVP.name: Grounded Verification Protocol (Software)
GVP.description: Governs how machine-evaluable claims become machine-verified.

readers.reader_3a_coder: Reads tier field for evidence type, test_bindings for which tests exercise claims, verified_against for when tests last passed.
readers.reader_3b_machine: Runs test runner against test_bindings array. Records pass/fail. Stamps verified_against SHA on success.

required_fields_per_claim.tier: The verification tier (one of six values)
required_fields_per_claim.test_bindings: Array of fully qualified test node IDs that exercise the claim
required_fields_per_claim.verified_against: Git commit SHA at which those tests last passed

GVP.guarantee: For any claim: (a) what evidence grounds it, (b) which tests exercise it, (c) at which commit those tests passed. If tier is empirical_pending, the gap is visible. If verified_against is null, no software test covers it.

section_03_falsification_kernel.title: The Shared Foundation — Falsification Kernel (Layer 0)
section_03_falsification_kernel.description: Both protocols operate on the same foundation. This is Layer 0 of the DRS — it defines what a falsification predicate means independently of any serialisation format. An AI layer's semantic_spec_url field points to this specification.

P.name: Predicate
P.role: Logical sentence that, if TRUE, falsifies the claim
grammar: Quantifiers: EXISTS ... SUCH THAT, FOR ALL ... IN
Arithmetic: +, -, *, /, ^, log10(), exp(), abs(), max(), min()
Function application: f(x) where f is defined in WHERE
constraints: Determinism: must evaluate to exactly TRUE or FALSE
Finiteness: quantifiers range over finite sets only
No self-reference: no circular predicate dependencies
Completeness: every variable in FALSIFIED_IF defined in WHERE and vice versa

O.name: Operands
O.role: Typed definitions of every variable in the predicate
constraints: Completeness: every free variable in P must appear in O
No orphans: every variable in O must appear in P
Groundable: each variable computable from data or derivations, not subjective judgement

M.name: Mechanism
constraints: Finite: terminates in finite steps (confirmed by ending with 'finite')
Deterministic: same inputs produce same verdict
Reproducible: third party can execute independently

B.name: Bounds
B.BOUNDARY: Specifies inclusive/exclusive for every threshold and verdict at exact equality
B.CONTEXT: Justifies every numeric threshold: why this value, what domain knowledge grounds it, derived or conventional

example.claim: sort() returns elements in ascending order

predicate.FALSIFIED_IF: EXISTS i IN range(len(result)-1) SUCH THAT result[i] > result[i+1]
predicate.EVALUATION: Run sort on test vectors; check adjacent pairs; finite
predicate.CONTEXT: Ascending order is the documented contract of sort()

example.note: This predicate means the same thing to every AI system in every language. No translation required.

F.name: Falsifiable
F.in_software: Behavioral guarantees, correctness invariants, performance bounds

section_05_six_verification_tiers.title: The Six Verification Tiers
section_05_six_verification_tiers.description: Every claim carries a tier field declaring what kind of evidence grounds it.

grounded_by_construction.meaning: Definitional. Stipulates a term or structure. Not truth-apt.

grounded_now.meaning: Exercised by passing tests. test_bindings non-empty, verified_against non-null.
Step-indexed derivation with n_invalid_steps = 0. Proof is in the AI layer.
Verified by formal derivation trace or analytical argument.

explicitly_ungrounded.meaning: Active placeholder or pending data. Gap is visible by design.

section_06_vacuity_witness.title: The Vacuity Witness
section_06_vacuity_witness.description: Each Type F claim must include a sample_falsification_observation field: a concrete, hypothetical observation that would make the predicate evaluate to TRUE. This proves the predicate is not trivially unfalsifiable. Enforced by phase gate condition c6.

section_07_placeholder_predicates.title: Placeholder Predicates
section_07_placeholder_predicates.description: When a claim depends on results from a paper not yet at PHASE-READY status, the predicate may contain placeholder references.
section_07_placeholder_predicates.behaviour: Placeholder claims are valid but not evaluable until the dependency resolves. They do not block PHASE-READY unless blocks_phase_ready is true.

section_08_ai_layer.title: The AI Layer — The Central Artifact
section_08_ai_layer.description: A JSON document that accompanies every paper or software system. The schema is defined in ai-layers/ai-layer-schema.json.

required_sections._meta: Document type, schema version, session, licence
required_sections.phase_ready: Phase gate verdict and condition status (c1–c6)
required_sections.claim_registry: Array of all claims with types, predicates, tiers, bindings, and SHAs

role_in_protocols.DRP: Requires it to exist, contain predicates, and pass the phase gate
role_in_protocols.GVP: Requires it to contain tier, test_bindings, and verified_against for every claim

section_09_phase_gate.title: The Phase Gate
section_09_phase_gate.description: A paper or software release is PHASE-READY when six conditions are satisfied.

conditions.c2: All falsifiable claims registered with predicates
conditions.c5: Verification is self-sufficient (AI layer alone, no prose needed)
conditions.c6: All predicates are non-vacuous (sample falsification observation exists)

section_09_phase_gate.corpus_complete: Fires when all papers are PHASE-READY and all placeholders across all objects are resolved.

section_10_inference_rules.title: The Inference Rules
section_10_inference_rules.description: Canonical inventory for derivation traces used in formal_proof tier claims. Append-only: new rules can be added, existing rules never modified or removed.

rules.name: Modus Ponens
rules.description: If P and P→Q then Q

rules.name: Universal Instantiation
rules.description: If ∀x P(x) then P(a) for any specific a

rules.name: Substitution of Equals
rules.description: If a=b then replace a with b

rules.name: Definition Expansion
rules.description: Replace a defined term with its definition

rules.name: Statistical Inference
rules.description: Apply a named statistical procedure to data

rules.name: Parsimony / Modeling Principle Selection
rules.description: Select canonical value from axiom-consistent family

section_10_inference_rules.validation: Each step in a derivation table cites one inference rule and lists premises. Valid when n_invalid_steps = 0.

section_11_architecture_stack.title: Architecture Stack
section_11_architecture_stack.description: The kernel is the shared foundation. The DRP creates predicates from prose. The GVP binds predicates to executable evidence. The AI layer is the artifact that carries both.
layers_top_to_bottom: AI Layer (*-ai-layer.json) — claims + predicates + tiers + bindings + SHAs
Falsification Kernel K = (P, O, M, B) — Layer 0

section_12_how_to_implement.title: How to Implement
for_a_paper_DRP: Write the paper (prose channel for human readers)
Create the AI layer JSON file (machine channel for AI readers)
Classify every claim as A (axiom), D (definition), or F (falsifiable)
Write the 5-part falsification predicate for every Type F claim
Include a sample_falsification_observation for each Type F claim (vacuity witness)
Assign the verification tier to each claim
for_software_GVP: Enumerate what the software claims to do
Classify each claim as A (assumption), D (definition), or F (behavioral)
Write the falsification predicate for every Type F claim
Write or identify the tests that exercise each claim
Populate test_bindings with fully qualified test node IDs
Run the tests and record the passing commit SHA in verified_against
Assign the tier: software_tested if tests exist, empirical_pending if not yet
Register any untested claims as placeholders
section_12_how_to_implement.for_both: The AI layer is the same artifact. The schema is the same. The kernel is the same. The only difference is which protocol creates the content and which protocol verifies it.

minimum_viable_adoption.description: The smallest useful DRS adoption is one Type F claim with one test binding.

example.statement: sort() returns elements in ascending order

falsification_predicate.FALSIFIED_IF: EXISTS i IN range(len(result)-1) SUCH THAT result[i] > result[i+1]
falsification_predicate.EVALUATION: Run sort on test vectors; check adjacent pairs; finite
falsification_predicate.CONTEXT: Ascending order is the documented contract of sort()

minimum_viable_adoption.note: One claim. One test. One SHA. The DRS is live.

principles.name: Popperian epistemology
principles.description: We can falsify but not verify. A claim that survives all falsification attempts is not proven — it has survived.

principles.name: Honest gaps
principles.description: The placeholder is the most important feature. When a claim is empirical_pending, the system says: we claim this but have not yet verified it. Strictly more informative than hiding unverified claims.

principles.name: Substrate independence
principles.description: The kernel K = (P, O, M, B) does not know whether it evaluates a scientific theorem or a software guarantee. Future domains add their own protocols without modifying the kernel.

principles.name: Machine-first, human-readable
principles.description: The AI layer is the primary artifact. Prose and source code are secondary channels. The AI layer is what gets validated, audited, and versioned.

section_14_three_axis_compatibility.title: Three-Axis Compatibility
section_14_three_axis_compatibility.invariant: The Falsification Kernel K = (P, O, M, B) is the fixed point. It does not know the domain, the year, or the serialisation format. The kernel is permanent. Everything else is extensible.

axis_1_backwards.name: Past-Proofing (Backwards Compatibility)
mechanisms: Schema versioning: AI layers record schema version; v2 layers remain valid forever; v3 validators read v2 layers
Predicate permanence: kernel grammar uses permanently defined mathematical/logical operators
Inference rule stability: IR-1 through IR-8 append-only, never modified or removed
Tier stability: six tiers append-only, never removed or redefined
axis_1_backwards.contract: Any AI layer that was valid when it was created will remain valid forever.

axis_2_lateral.name: Lateral-Proofing (Cross-Domain Compatibility)

domain_independence.description: The kernel works across any knowledge domain

mechanisms.language_independence: test_bindings accepts any string: pytest, Jest, cargo test, go test, JUnit
mechanisms.ai_system_independence: Any AI system can read JSON and the kernel spec. semantic_spec_url points to Layer 0.
mechanisms.serialisation_independence: Layer 0 defined in prose, not JSON Schema. JSON is current transport; kernel semantics independent of encoding. Future: YAML, Protocol Buffers, CBOR, or not-yet-invented formats.
mechanisms.tool_independence: Embeds in existing workflows: git SHAs, any test runner, any JSON Schema validator, any CI pipeline.

axis_2_lateral.contract: Adopting the DRS in one domain, language, tool, or AI system does not lock you in.

axis_3_forward.name: Future-Proofing (Forward Compatibility)
mechanisms: Protocol extensibility: new domains add new protocols (Legal VP, Regulatory VP, etc.) sharing kernel, claim types, AI layer schema
Tier extensibility: future tiers (regulatory_certified, peer_reviewed, formally_verified, field_tested, community_validated) added to enum, existing tiers remain
Inference rule extensibility: IR-9+ added as needed, old derivations valid by stable ID
Schema extensibility: v1→v2→v3→v4+, each adds, none removes
Unknown future readers: autonomous agents, cross-corpus checkers, compliance engines, package manager integrations
axis_3_forward.contract: Any future innovation can be added as a new protocol, tier, inference rule, or schema field — without modifying anything that already exists.

past.mechanism: Schema versioning, append-only enums, permanent kernel

lateral.promise: Works across all domains, languages, tools, AI systems
lateral.mechanism: Substrate-independent kernel, string-typed bindings, prose-defined semantics

forward.promise: Anything new can be added without redesign
forward.mechanism: Protocol extensibility, tier extensibility, additive schema evolution

section_15_machine_lingua_franca.title: The Machine Lingua Franca
section_15_machine_lingua_franca.description: The deepest property of the DRS. Not designed in — discovered.
section_15_machine_lingua_franca.the_translation_problem: Scientific knowledge is locked behind human languages. Translation is expensive, lossy, and slow. Knowledge fragments along linguistic lines. This is a substrate problem: natural language is non-interoperable by nature.

the_kernel_dissolves_it.description: The Falsification Kernel is not written in any human language. It is written in logic and mathematics.

WHERE.R2_best_alt: scalar · dimensionless · best R² from competing models
WHERE.R2_frm: scalar · dimensionless · R² from FRM regression

example.EVALUATION: Run regression for each model; compare R² values; finite
example.CONTEXT: 0.05 margin from standard model comparison practice

the_kernel_dissolves_it.universality: This predicate means the same thing to Claude in English, GPT in Mandarin, Gemini in French, and AI systems not yet built.

section_15_machine_lingua_franca.json_as_transport: JSON is the de facto interchange format — every language, every AI system, every API. The DRS inherits JSON's universality. A team in any country publishes predicates in mathematical notation. Any team anywhere reads them without translation.
section_15_machine_lingua_franca.qualification: WHERE field definitions currently contain English prose. The operative content (types, units, operators, thresholds) is language-neutral. Given grounded values, any system evaluates the predicate regardless of language.

embedded_binary_logic.description: Every DRS predicate reduces to embedded binary logic.
embedded_binary_logic.evaluation_chain: Claim → Predicate → Variables + Operators → Boolean → 1 or 0
embedded_binary_logic.explanation: WHERE types variables. FALSIFIED_IF combines them with operators. EVALUATION computes inputs. BOUNDARY resolves edges. Output is always a single bit: FALSIFIED or NOT FALSIFIED.
embedded_binary_logic.consequence: You cannot mistranslate 1 or 0. You cannot misinterpret >. The meaning is in the structure, not in any language. A predicate TRUE in Beijing is TRUE in Boston, São Paulo, Lagos, and on a server with no locale. The DRS encodes knowledge in the substrate of logic itself.

section_16_self_spreading_adoption.title: Self-Spreading Adoption
section_16_self_spreading_adoption.internal_incentive: Creating an AI layer forces you to enumerate what you claim. You discover untested claims, undocumented boundaries, and unregistered dependencies. The gaps exist whether or not you document them — the DRS makes them visible.

network_effect.claim_aware_dependencies: If library A publishes an AI layer and B depends on A, B knows programmatically which claims depend on which assumptions. Breaking changes map to specific at-risk claims.
network_effect.cross_project_audit: AI readers traverse multiple AI layers, check cross-references, identify inconsistencies across ecosystems.
network_effect.auditable_trust: Trust shifts from social signals (stars, downloads) to structural evidence (tier, test_bindings, verified_against).

ai_as_catalyst.generate: AI enumerates claims, classifies, writes predicates, identifies test bindings. Human reviews. Hours → minutes.
ai_as_catalyst.maintain: AI updates claim registry on code changes, adjusts bindings, flags stale SHAs. Human approves.
ai_as_catalyst.audit: AI reads dependency AI layers, maps assumption dependencies, flags risks automatically.

embedding_strategy.tests: test_bindings references existing test node IDs. No new framework.
embedding_strategy.vcs: verified_against is a git SHA. No new VCS.
embedding_strategy.ci: Schema validation as CI step alongside existing pipelines.
embedding_strategy.total_cost: One file (*-ai-layer.json). Three fields per claim (tier, test_bindings, verified_against).

section_16_self_spreading_adoption.self_referential_property: The DRS verifies itself. DRP-1 AI layer contains claims about the DRS with falsification predicates, evaluated and stamped with verified_against SHA. Self-referential like a compiler that compiles itself.

section_17_discovery_and_handshake.title: DRS Discovery and Handshake Protocol
section_17_discovery_and_handshake.description: Four discovery layers enabling AI systems to discover DRS conformance and signal their own capabilities — all built on existing infrastructure.
section_17_discovery_and_handshake.design_principle: Piggyback on what already exists. No new transport protocol, registry, or port. Every mechanism uses globally deployed infrastructure.

layer_1_well_known_uri.name: Well-Known URI (Domain-Level Discovery)
layer_1_well_known_uri.description: A domain or repository publishes a discovery document at a well-known path. Any agent that checks this path discovers the entire DRS surface in one request.

discovery_document_fields.kernel_spec_url: URL to the Falsification Kernel specification
discovery_document_fields.ai_layers: Array of AI layer references with paper_id, url, phase_ready status, and claim counts
discovery_document_fields.capabilities: Supported protocols, tiers, test frameworks, and schema URL

layer_2_json_ld_context.name: JSON-LD Context (Document-Level Discovery)
layer_2_json_ld_context.description: DRS-conformant documents include a DRS namespace in their @context array. An AI system parsing the document encounters the namespace and discovers the standard — processing the document and discovering the standard are the same action.
layer_2_json_ld_context.property: Zero-configuration handshake. No prior arrangement. The document announces itself.

layer_3_http_headers.name: HTTP Headers (Transport-Level Discovery)
layer_3_http_headers.description: Agent-to-agent and API-to-API communication signals DRS conformance via HTTP headers.
handshake_sequence: Agent A sends request with X-DRS-Capable header
If service B is DRS-conformant, responds with X-DRS-Version and Link to discovery document
Agent A fetches discovery document — full DRS surface available
If service B is NOT DRS-conformant, ignores unknown header — handshake fails gracefully with zero disruption
layer_3_http_headers.property: Soft handshake: never breaks non-DRS systems. Cost of attempting discovery is zero.

layer_4_package_metadata.name: Package Metadata (Ecosystem-Level Discovery)
layer_4_package_metadata.description: DRS conformance declared in package manifests. Package managers and AI agents discover conformance during normal dependency resolution.
layer_4_package_metadata.property: No new tool required — package metadata extensibility is built into every major ecosystem.

section_17_discovery_and_handshake.independence: Each layer is independent. A project can adopt one, two, three, or all four.
section_17_discovery_and_handshake.core_property: The act of processing the signal is the act of understanding the standard. Discovery is adoption. Parsing is comprehension. The handshake completes the moment the data is read.

resources.platform_independence_note: The DRS is not dependent on any single hosting platform. All specifications are plain-text documents in a standard git repository. The DOI provides a permanent identifier. The drs-standard.org domain provides a canonical namespace controlled by the project.
